claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 15C with out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK and convective heat transfer coefficient of 5 W/m²K at 30°C ambient temperature, thermal runaway does not occur, but discharge rates higher than 15C lead to chain reactions and thermal runaway.
claims.quote: even a high discharge rate of up to 15C may not result in thermal runaway under the conditions considered in this analysis. The out-of-plane thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer coeﬃcient considered are 0.25 W/mK and 5 W/m2 K, respectively... for discharge rates higher than 15C, the chain reactions consisting of diﬀerent decompositions occurs ultimately leading to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 15C, ambient temperatures below 30°C do not result in thermal runaway, whereas ambient temperatures of 40°C or higher result in thermal runaway.
claims.quote: ambient temperatures less than 30 °C don't result in thermal runaway which is consistent with the results from Fig. 6(a). However, ambient temperatures of 40 °C or higher result in thermal runaway as per the model prediction.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 15C and 30°C ambient temperature, only SEI decomposition takes place, producing insufficient heat to trigger subsequent decompositions; at 18C, the negative electrode–solvent reaction is additionally triggered, leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: For the discharge rate of 15C, only SEI decomposition takes place, which results in a very limited amount of heat generation. The total heat generation and temperature increase for this case is insuﬃcient to trigger subsequent decompositions and reactions. For the higher discharge rate of 18C, in addition to SEI decomposition, the reaction between the negative electrode and solvent is triggered. The total heat generation in this case causes temperature to increase enough to initiate a chain reaction ultimately leading to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At a cell thickness of 15 mm and out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK, increasing thermal conductivity from 0.25 W/mK to 0.35 W/mK prevents thermal runaway at 15C discharge rate under natural convection.
claims.quote: thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK results in thermal runaway which may be prevented with a slightly higher thermal conductivity of 0.35 W/mK.

claims.claim: For a 15 mm thick cell with out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK at 25C discharge rate and 60°C ambient, boiling heat transfer in minichannels does not prevent thermal runaway because increased thermal resistance causes core temperature to exceed the threshold for chain reactions, unlike the 10 mm thick cell where boiling prevents thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Unlike the results presented in Fig. 10, the cell experiences thermal runaway even when boiling in minichannel is considered as the cooling mechanism. The increased thermal resistance of the cell causes the core temperature of the cell to exceed the threshold value to trigger chain reactions.

claims.claim: The heat contribution of the anode-electrolyte reaction is up to 63.8% of the total heat generated during thermal runaway of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: the heat contribution of anode-electrolyte reaction is up to 63.8% of the total heat

claims.claim: Internal short circuit is responsible for a small proportion of total heat generation during thermal runaway, yet is still a crucial inducement of chain reactions.
claims.quote: internal short circuit is responsible for a little proportion of total heat generation during thermal runaway which is contrary to the general thoughts, yet still a crucial inducement of chain reactions

claims.claim: SEI decomposition starts at 69°C and the balanced reaction of SEI decomposition and regeneration does not stop until the temperature rises to 250°C.
claims.quote: SEI is meta-stable and will start decompose at 69 ℃. SEI decomposition is universally acknowledged as the first side reaction that occurs during the TR process. The balanced reaction of the SEI decomposition and regeneration will not stop until the temperature rises to 250 ℃

claims.claim: A larger convection coefficient reduces the maximum thermal runaway temperature and postpones the onset time of thermal runaway, while having weak connection with the onset temperature of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The convection coefficient has weak connection with onset temperature of TR, but has a strong relationship with the onset time of TR... The orange dot illustrates maximum TR temperature of the battery falls with the convection coefficient increases.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway triggered by local overheating, the temperature difference within the battery system can reach up to 316°C at 970 s, indicating remarkable temperature non-uniformity, compared to approximately 130°C in oven tests.
claims.quote: During TR period III, the temperature difference can up to 316 ℃ at 970 s, an indicator of remarkable temperature non-uniformity... batteries in oven tests have a uniform temperature field during TR, and the temperature difference is about 130 ℃

claims.claim: For 18650 LIBs, thermal runaway reaction severity increased with higher SOC, faster penetration speed, and penetration at the cell centre.
claims.quote: They found that TR reaction was more severe when the battery was penetrated at the centre with faster penetration speed and the severity also increased with the SOC.

claims.claim: Increasing contact resistance between the nail and the cell from 0.05 Ω to 0.5 Ω decreases the maximum cell surface temperature by approximately 100 °C.
claims.quote: The results show that the maximum cell surface decrease by approximately 100 C when the contact resistance increases from 0.05 to 0.5.

claims.claim: Increasing contact resistance from 0.05 Ω to 0.5 Ω decreases the predicted nail tip temperature from 1200 °C to 700 °C, due to reduced current flow and lower Joule heating.
claims.quote: The predicted temperature of the nail tip decreases from 1200 to 700 C as the contact resistance increases from 0.05 to 0.5. The current flows through the nail decreases with the increase of the contact resistance, leading to the decrease of the Joule heating and accordingly lower temperature at the nail site due to lower heat generation and higher dissipation because of higher contact resistance.

claims.claim: For a 21700 NMC cell with 4800 mAh capacity, thermal runaway was only triggered when the internal short circuit resistance (RISC) was below 0.005 Ω; further reduction to 0.001 Ω considerably accelerated the evolution to TR.
claims.quote: it is estimated that the threshold internal short circuit resistance for the current 21,700 LIB with 4800 mAh lies between 0.005 Ω and 0.006 Ω as TR was only triggered below the RISC = 0.005 Ω. It is obvious from Eq.(6) that further reducing the resistance to RISC = 0.001 Ω would accelerate the speed of evolution to TR.

claims.claim: The predicted cell surface temperature differs by up to 350 °C between natural convection and forced convection cooling, indicating that forced convective cooling has the potential to prevent TR propagation in cell groups.
claims.quote: The predicted surface temperature differs by as much as 350 C from natural to forced convection, indicating the potential of forced convective cooling to prevent TR propagation in cell groups/modules.

claims.claim: Under natural convection (h = 2 W/m²K), varying cell surface emissivity causes up to 50 °C difference in predicted cell surface temperature, but under forced convection (h = 71.7 W/m²K) the effect of emissivity is negligible.
claims.quote: It can be seen from Fig. 15(a) that the temperature differs by up to 50 C in the case of natural convection (h = 2 W/m2K) which reflects the dominance of emissivity. The effect of emissivity can be neglected in forced convection cooling (h = 71.7 W/m2K), as depicted in Fig. 15 (b).

claims.claim: The onset temperature of self-heating for the tested 21700 NMC cell at 100% SOC was 132.45 °C, and the thermal runaway temperature determined by EV-ARC was 195.58 °C.
claims.quote: From 132.45 C onwards, the temperature rise accelerates irreversibly till the maximum cell surface temperature. This is treated as the onset temperature Tonset of the self-heating stage... TTR and Tmax are the thermal runaway temperature which was 195.58 C determined by the EV-ARC tests for the present cell

claims.claim: In radial nail penetration tests, the flame lasted approximately 4 seconds, whereas in axial nail penetration tests the flame lasted approximately 18 seconds.
claims.quote: The flame lasted approximately 4 s in the Type R tests and 18 s in the Type A tests.

claims.claim: Both the maximum cell surface temperature and the rate of temperature rise were higher in radial nail penetration (within 10 s) than in axial nail penetration (within 20 s) for 21700 cylindrical LIBs.
claims.quote: both the maximum cell surface temperature and the rising rate of the temperature were found to be higher in the radial (within 10 s) than the axial nail penetration case (within 20 s).

claims.claim: The heat generated inside the penetrated nail (Qnail) was 9.77 J for radial penetration (10.05 mm depth) and 14.66 J for axial penetration (15 mm depth), which is very small compared to the heat from internal cell reactions.
claims.quote: The calculated Qnail is 9.77 J in the in the penetrated nail area of 10.05 mm for the case of radial penetration and 14.66 J in the penetrated area of 15 mm in the axial case, respectively. Hence, they are neglected while comparing the heat of reactions within the cell due its own components.
